messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the apply access-list (to VLAN) command do?

The apply access-list (to VLAN) command applies an ACL to the VLAN identified by the current VLAN context. The no form of this command removes application of the ACL from the VLAN identified by the current VLAN context. Specifies the ACL type: ip for IPv4, IPv6 for IPv6, or mac for M AC ACL. Specifies the ACL name. Selects the inbound (ingress) traffic direction. Selects the outbound (egress) traffic direction. NOTE For HPE Aruba Networking 6000 and 6100 switch series, the outbound (egress) traffic directio n is supported only for MAC ACLs. • Each ACL of a given type can be applied to the same VLAN once in each direction. Therefore, using the apply access-list command on a VLAN with an already-applied ACL of the same type, will replace the applied ACL. Syntax: apply access-list {ip|IPv6|mac} <ACL-NAME> {in|out}.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show resources command do?

The show resources command on the HPE Aruba Networking 8100 and 8360 Switch Series, shows hardware resource consumption on the switch. Resource data is updated every 10 seconds. Hardware resource consumption information is shown for: Public ACL and Policy hardware resource commands 215 • TCAM entries • Policers • L4 Port Ranges Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. The widths for show resources can have features combined (IPv4 + IPv6) into one TCAM lookup. Therefore, the table widths for each ACL/classifier policy type are variable depending on what is applied. Syntax: show resources [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip local-proxy-arp exclude {<ip-address>} command do?

The ip local-proxy-arp exclude {<ip-address>} command enables exemption of a specific IP address from local proxy ARP for the specified interface. This allows specified IP addresses to bypass local proxy ARP processing on the interface. The maximum IP addresses can be exempted from Local Proxy ARP per interface are 256. The maximum IP addresses can be exempted from Local proxy ARP across all interfaces are 256. The no version of this command disables exemption of a specific IP address from local proxy ARP for the specified interface, while local proxy ARP remains configured on the interface. This allows specified IP addresses to be processed by local proxy ARP on the interface. Public ip local-proxy-arp exclude 225 IP address to exclude from local proxy ARP. Syntax: no ip local-proxy-arp exclude {<ip-address>}.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude {<IPv6-address>} command do?

The IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude {<IPv6-address>} command enables IP exemption for local proxy ND for the specified interface and IPv6 address. When configured, the switch will not respond to neighbor solicitation (NS) messages for the specified IPv6 address. The maximum IPv6 addresses can be exempted from Local Proxy ND per interface are 256. The maximum IPv6 addresses can be exempted from Local proxy ND across all interfaces are 256. The no version of this command disables IP exemption for local proxy ND for the specified interface and IPv6 address, while local proxy ND remains configured on the interface. After executing this command, the switch will respond to neighbor solicitation (NS) messages for the specified IPv6 address. Public IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude 228 IPv6 address to exclude from local proxy ND. Syntax: no IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude {<IPv6-address>}.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip proxy-arp command do?

The ip proxy-arp command enables proxy ARP for the specified Layer 3 interface. Proxy ARP is supported on Layer 3 physical interfaces, LAG interfaces, and VLAN interfaces. It is disabled by default. To enable proxy ARP on an interface, routing must be enabled on that interface. The no form of this command disables proxy ARP for the specified interface. Syntax: no ip proxy-arp.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show arp inspection interface command do?

The show arp inspection interface command shows the current configuration of dynamic ARP inspection on an interface. Specifies the interface. Specifies the VLAN ID. Range: 1 to 4094. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Syntax: show arp inspection interface [<IFNAME>] [vlan <VLAN-ID>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the bfd <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The bfd <IPv4-ADDR> command enables BFD under VRRP for the specified IP address. BFD is asynchronous and echo mode is supported. The no form of this command disables BFD under VRRP for the specified IP address. Specifies the address on which to enable BFD in IPv4 format (x. x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Public bfd <IPv4-ADDR> 287. Syntax: no bfd <IPv4-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the bfd all-interfaces command do?

The bfd all-interfaces command enables BFD on all OSPFv2 or OSPFv3 interfaces. The no form of this command disables BFD on all active OSPFv2/OSPFv3 or IPv4/IPv6 interfaces, excluding those on which BFD was enabled at the interface level with the commands ip ospf bfd and IPv6 o spfv3 bfd. Syntax: no bfd all-interfaces.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the bfd echo disable command do?

The bfd echo disable command disables support for BFD echo packets. Echo packet support is enabled by default. Public bfd echo disable 294 The no form of this command enables support for BFD echo packets. NOTE Toggling this feature on 8100, 8325, 8360, 9300, 9300S, 10040 switches may cause route flapping. NOTE BFD IPv6 Echo is not supported. Syntax: no bfd echo disable.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR> command sets the source IPv4 address for BFD echo packets. This address is used in all echo sessions. NOTE The source IP address must not be on the same network segment as any switch interface, otherwise a large number of ICMP redirect packets may be sent by the remote device, causing network congestion. The no form of this command removes the source IPv4 address for BFD echo packets, which causes the switch to stop sending echo packets. When a valid value is set, all sessions with a peer that is capable of receiving echo packets, will start transmitting echo packets. BFD control sessions continue to run concurrently with echo packets. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR>. Syntax: no bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command do?

The bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command sets the minimum time interval between received BFD echo packets. Public bfd min-echo-receive-interval 297 The no form of this command removes the configured BFD echo packets interval. If the interval is not set, the default interval is used. NOTE BFD IPv6 Echo is not supported. Specifies the minimum reception interval in milliseconds. A valu e of 0 means that the switch does not support reception of BFD echo packets. Range: 0, 50 to 1000. Default: 500. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: no bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command do?

The bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command sets the minimum time interval between received BFD control packets on an interface. The no form of this command removes the configured BFD minimum interval on an interface. If the interval is not set, the default interval is used. Specifies the minimum receive interval in milliseconds. A value of 0 means that the switch does not support reception of BFD c ontrol packets. Range: 500 to 20000. Default: 3000. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: no bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor fall-over command do?

The neighbor fall-over command enables BGP fast peering session deactivation. When neighbor fall-over is configured, the BGP process monitors the RIB and if the route to peer is not present in the routing table, it immediately deactivates the peer session without waiting for the hold down timer. It is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables this feature. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDR> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Enables BGP to register with BFD to receive fast peering session deactivation messages from BFD. You can either configure BFD support for BGP per neighbor or peer-group. The no form of this command disables BGP for BFD. NOTE Multihop BFD is not supported for BGP. <IPv4-ADDR> Specifies the neighbor address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vrf <VRF-NAME> command do?

The vrf <VRF-NAME> command creates a VRF instance named <VRF-NAME> and then enters its context. Use default for <VRF-NAME> to enter the default VRF configure context. Except for the default VRF, the no form of the command deletes the named VRF instance and any IP configuration for interfaces or SVI linked to default VRF. The default VRF cannot be deleted and a warning is given if attempted. To erase the Route-Distinguisher and Route-Targets, enter the default VRF context and delete them manually one by one. Public vrf 500 Specifies the VRF name. Range: Up to 32 alphanumeric chara cters. The mgmt VRF cannot be used. <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: no vrf <VRF-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-bidir command do?

The ip pim-bidir command enables or disables PIM-Bidir in the designated interface. This command works in the interface context, including loopback. The IP address must be configured on the interface to enable BIDIR-PIM. Enables PIM Bidirectional on the interface. Disables PIM Bidirectional on the interface. Syntax: ip pim-bidir [enable | disable].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE> command changes the maximum time, in seconds, before the router transmits the initial PIM hello message on the current interface. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets it to default. The default value is 5. Public ip pim-bidir hello-delay 503 Configures the given value as the hello interval. Range: <0-5>. <DELAY-VALUE>. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command changes the frequency at which the router transmits PIM hello messages on the current subnet. The router uses hello packets to inform neighbor routers of its presence and to comput the hello hold time. Hello hold time informs neighbor routers estimated wait time for the next hello packet from the router. Shortening the hello interval reduces the hello hold time. Default is set to 30 seconds. The no form of this command resets the frequency of PIM hello messages back to the default value on the current subnet. Public ip pim-bidir hello-interval 505 Configures the given value as hello interval. Range: 1-300 seco nds. • The default value (30 seconds) covers most deployment needs; change only for explicit convergence or timing requirements. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value>.

source_file: 8100/10_18/Command-line Interface Reference Guide For 8100 and 8360 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. In chatty networks where there is a chance of Hello packets being lost, configuring a higher hold time reduces the likelihood of adjacency loss. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-bidir override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command do?

The ip pim-bidir override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command configures the override interval that gets inserted into the Override Interval field of a LAN prune delay option. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets it to the default. The default value is 2500 milliseconds. Public ip pim-bidir override-interval 510 <INTERVAL-VALUE> Specifies override interval value. Range: 500-6000. Default: 2500. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 pim6-bidir lan-prune-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-bidir lan-prune-delay command enables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. With lan-prune-delay enabled, the router informs downstream neighbors on the wait time before pruning a flow after receiving a prune request. The no form of this command disables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. Syntax: no IPv6 pim6-bidir lan-prune-delay.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in command do?

The apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in command applies a policy to the current physical interface port or LAG or VLAN context. Subinterfaces are supported on interfaces and LAGs. Public apply policy (config-if, config-lag-if, config-if-... 560 a p p l y p o l i c y (c o n fi g - i f, c o n fi g - l a g - i f, c o n fi g - i f -... Only one direction of a policy can be applied to an interface or VLAN at a time, thus using the apply command on an interface or VLAN with an already-applied policy of the same direction will replace the currently applied policy. NOTE The VLAN context supports the in and out directions, which apply to both bridged and routed traffic. The Interface VLAN context only supports the routed-in direction which applies only to routed traffic. Syntax: no apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR> command configures a gateway address for the DHCP relay agent to use for DHCP requests. By default DHCP relay agent picks the lowest-numbered IP address on the interface. The no form of this command removes the gateway address. Specifies the IP address of the gateway in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Public ip bootp-gateway 784. Syntax: no ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command do?

The ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command configures a domain name that is appended to the DNS request. The domain can be either IPv4 or IPv6. By default, requests are forwarded on the default VRF. If a domain list is defined with the command ip dns domain-list, the domain name defined with this command is ignored. The no form of this command removes the domain name. Specifies the domain name to append to DNS requests. Length: 1 to 256 characters. Public ip dns domain-name 914 vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: no ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip dns host <HOST-NAME> command do?

The ip dns host <HOST-NAME> command associates a static IP address with a hostname. The DNS client returns this IP address instead of querying a DNS server for an IP address for the hostname. Up to six hosts can be defined. If no VRF is defined, the default VRF is used. The no form of this command removes a static IP address associated with a hostname. host <HOST-NAME> Specifies the name of a host. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexa decimal number from 0 to F. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax:
- ip dns host 915
- no ip dns host <HOST-NAME>.

syntax:
- ip dns host 915
- no ip dns host <HOST-NAME>

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip ecmp command do?

The show ip ecmp command displays the Equal Cost Multipath (ECMP) configuration. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Syntax: show ip ecmp [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the erps ring <RINGID> command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> command this command creates an ERPS ring with a given ID. The no form of this command removes all the configurations of the ring, including instances. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> This command configures the ERPS ring member port. An L2 interface in the switch is associated to one of the two member ports of an ERPS ring. In case of an interconnection node, only port0 is applicable for the sub-ring. The no form of this command removes the association of the ring port to the L2 interface on the switch. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <PORT0> Required, set port0 of the ring. Public erps ring <RINGID> <port0|port1> interface 941 Required, set port1 of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the erps ring <RINGID> hold-off-interval command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> hold-off-interval command specifies hold-off interval in units of 100 ms. If specified, a defect is not reported immediately. Instead, the hold-off timer is started. On expiration of the timer, if the defect still exists, it is reported to protection switching. The default value for hold-off timer is 0. The no form of this command removes the configured value of the hold-off interval and sets it to the default value of 0. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 Required, specifies the hold-off interval in units of 100 ms. De fault: 0. <RINGID> <100 milliseconds> On a common ERPS network, a physical ring can be configured with a single ERPS ring, and only one blocked port can be specified in the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> control-vlan command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> control-vlan command this command adds a control-channel VLAN to a ring instance. In an ERPS ring, the control VLANshould be used only to forward RAPS PDUs and not service packets. All the devices in an ERPS ring instance must be configured with the same control VLAN, and different ERPS ring instances must use different control VLANs. The no form of this command removes the control-channel VLAN of the ring instance. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <ID> <VID> This command adds descriptive information to help administrators and operators understand the purpose of a ring instance. 1-64 printable ASCII characters are allowed. The no form of this command removes the ring instance description. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command configures the default revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In revertive operation, after the conditions causing protection switching are cleared, traffic channels are restored to the recovered link blocking the RPL. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. The no form of this command configures non-revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In non-revertive operation, the traffic channels continue to use the RPL, if it has not failed, after conditions causing protection switching are cleared. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <ID> In ERPS, there is a central node called RPL Owner Node which blocks one of the ports to ensure that there is no loop formed for the Ethernet traffic. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command the R-APS messages transmitted by ERPS take the form of OAM PDUs as defined in G.8013. Each OAM PDU is transmitted at a specified level known as the Maintenance Entity Group (MEG) level. This command configures the level with which the ERPS packets must be transmitted. The no form of this command removes the configured MEG level and sets it to the default value of 7. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 Required, specifies the meg-level. Range: 0-7. Default: 7. <RINGID> <0-7> This command associates a sub-ring to a parent-ring and is required for the sub-ring to notify the parent- ring on change in topology. The no form of this command removes the parent ring identifier. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show evpn mac-ip evi command do?

The show evpn mac-ip evi command show the information about the EVPN MAC-IP for the particular EVPN instance. Specifies the EVPN instance ID. <EVI-ID> Shows the information about the MLD group memberships learned through EVPN. A default SMET route is identified by the Source and Group addresses set to 0: : 0 and is applicable for both IP versions. Specifies the EVPN instance ID. Range: 1-16777214. <EVI-ID> Shows the remote VTEPs MAC-IP binding. The state of the peer VTEP denotes whether VXLAN tunnel to the VTEP is Up or Down. Display information for all VRFs. Specify a VRF by VRF name (if no <VRF-NAME> is specifie d, the default VRF is implied. Shows all EVPN configurations. Configures the virtual MAC address for EVPN. The no form of this command removes the virtual MAC address configuration. Syntax: show evpn mac-ip evi <EVI-ID>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the feature-pack server command do?

The feature-pack server command if the switch is in cloud-managed feature pack mode, use this command to define the switch's feature pack profile. A switch in cloud-managed mode uses the information in this profile to contact the feature pack management server and download and install any allocated feature packs. If the subscription pool for the profile contains more than on e subscription block, specify the subscription block within that pool to be assigned. Configures the credentials used by the device to contact and au thenticate to the feature pack server. user <USER> The user name of a feature pack server account. password plaintext <PASSWORD> Select a mode for entering the feature pack server password. Syntax: feature-pack server.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the redundancy switchover command do?

The redundancy switchover command causes the switch to immediately switch over to the Standby Management Module. This command must be executed from the Active Management Module and will fail if the Standby Management Module is in a failed state or not present. Creates or enters the HPE Aruba Networking Central configuration context (config-hpe-anw-central). Public HPE Aruba Networking Central commands 1186 When a switch is able to connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the HPE Aruba Networking Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the HPE Aruba Networking Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to HPE Aruba Networking Central and will get disconnected again. Syntax: redundancy switchover.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip icmp unreachable command do?

The ip icmp unreachable command enables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages on the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. The unreachable host address originates from the failed packed. Default setting. The no form of this command disables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages from the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. This command does not prevent other hosts from sending an ICMP unreachable message. Syntax: no ip icmp unreachable.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the IGMP join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command unconfigures the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Specifies the name of the ACL. Syntax: no ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME> command do?

The ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME> command configures initial IGMP querier-wait-time value in seconds. The no form of this command sets the IGMP querier-wait-time to the default value of 260 seconds. Note that the wait timer can be configured to any numbers within the 1-300 second range. Configures IGMP querier-wait-time to desired value. <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME>. Syntax: no ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME>.

Values: 2 or 3. <VERSION>. Syntax: no ip igmp version <VERSION>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command do?

The ip igmp snooping all-vlans command enable IGMP snooping on all VLANs. This configuration setting enables IGMP snooping on all existing VLANs, as well as on any VLANs that are created after the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command is issued. Public ip igmp snooping all-vlans 1286 When IGMP snooping is enabled globally, IGMP snooping can not be configured on individual VLANs within the VLAN context of the command-line interface. The no form of this command disables global IGMP snooping, allows IGMP snooping to remain enabled at the VLAN level, and allows the configuration of VLAN-level IGMP snooping settings. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping all-vlans.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip tcp mss <VALUE> command do?

The ip tcp mss <VALUE> command sets the TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) MSS (Maximum Segment Size) for an interface. When configured, the MSS option in the TCP header is set in the TCP handshake for negotiation during connection establishment. Once the TCP session is established, changing the TCP MSS value on the interface will not affect the existing session. The no form of this command removes the TCP MSS configuration from the interface. NOTE TCP MSS is supported on ROP, SVI, subinterface, and L3 GRE tunnel interface. Specifies the IPv4 TCP MSS in bytes. Range: 68 to 65495. <VALUE>. Syntax:
- no ip tcp mss <VALUE>
- ip tcp mss 1324.

syntax:
- no ip tcp mss <VALUE>
- ip tcp mss 1324

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip unnumbered <ifname> command do?

The ip unnumbered <ifname> command the IP unnumbered feature allows the switch to process IP packets without configuring a unique IP address on an interface by telling the router to use the IP address of another selected interface as the address for that link. Once set, this port will borrow the user-configured primary IPv4 address from an lender interface and use that IP address in its L3 control plane exchange. The no version of this command removes IP unnumbered support for this port. Once unset, this port will remove borrowed IPv4 address. Name of the interface. Syntax: no ip unnumbered <ifname>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip directed-broadcast command do?

The ip directed-broadcast command turns on IP Directed Broadcast for the specified interface. The no form of this command turns it off. This command is disabled by default. NOTE IP Directed Broadcast is not supported on subinterfaces on 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8360 and 10000 Switch series. Public ip directed-broadcast 1407. Syntax: no ip directed-broadcast.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip neighbor-flood command do?

The ip neighbor-flood command enables VLAN flooding for the specified VLAN interface when a neighbor link goes down. The no form of this command disables VLAN flooding for the specified VLAN interface. Displays the status of IP Neighbor Flood on the specified interface along with other interface-related attributes. Specifies the interface name (for example, vlan30). Optional. <IFNAME> Displays the interfaces on which IP Neighbor Flood is enabled. Displays the current running configuration. Configures custom IPv6 route prefix lengths for the exact prefix match tables of the switch. The switch must be rebooted to apply the change. NOTE Following the reboot, IPv6 prefix priorities will remain in a pending state until at least one route is learned. Syntax: ip neighbor-flood.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME> command do?

The ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME> command creates an IP Service Level Agreement (SLA) profile and switches to the config-ip-sla context. The no form of this command deletes an IP-SLA profile. By default, all profile use the default VRF (default). Specifies an IP-SLA profile name. Length: 1 to 64 characters. <IP-SLA-NAME> Selects the IP-SLA responder. The responder can be configured for udp-echo, tcp-connect, udp-jitter-voip type. It requires the SLA name, SLA type, and port number as arguments. The no form of this command removes the IP-SLA responder. Specifies the IP-SLA responder name. Length: 1 to 64 chara cters. <SLA-NAME> udp-echo tcp-connect Enables responder for udp-echo probes. Selects TCP connect as the IP-SLA test mechanism. udp-jitter-voip Selects VOIP jitter as the IP-SLA test mechanism. Syntax: ip-sla responder <SLA-NAME> (udp-echo | tcp-connect | udp-jitter-voip).

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command do?

The ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command sets the local IP address of a GRE tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to a GRE tunnel. Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading ze ros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192. 168.5.100. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK>. Syntax: no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command do?

The ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command enables the IPv6-only-preferred mode on the VLAN interface, and is also supported on the OOBM interface. Once enabled, the DHCP client prioritizes a IPv6 address over an IPv4 address. To obtain a DHCPv4 address, the IPv6-only-preferred command must be disabled, and the DHCPv4 client must be restarted. This command is disabled by default. NOTE Ensure the server supports the IPv6-only-preferred mode option, as the command will not work unless supported by the server. The no form of this command disables IPv6-only-preferred mode. NOTE The IPv6-only-preferred mode command must be configured prior to the ip dhcp command, otherwise it will not take effect. n/a Public ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred 1497. Syntax: no ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command do?

The IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command controls the M fLAGsetting in RAs the router transmits on the current interface. Enable the M flag to indicate that hosts can obtain IP address through DHCPv6. The M flag is disabled by default. The no form of this command turns off (disables) the M flag. 0 0 0 1 Indicates that no information is available via DHCPv6. Indicates that other configuratio n information is available via DH CPv6. Examples of such informat ion are DNS-related informatio n or information on other servers within the network. Public IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag 1516 M flag O flag 1 1 0 1 Indicates that addresses are avail able via Dynamic Host Configura tion Protocol (DHCPv6). If the M flag is set, the O flag is redundant and can be ignored be cause DHCPv6 will return all avai lable configuration information. Syntax: no IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 nd ra max-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ra max-interval command configures the maximum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. The interval is calculated to be a value between the current max-interval and min-interval settings. The no form of this command returns the setting to its default, provided the default value is less than the default lifetime value. Specifies the maximum advertisement time in seconds. Range: 4-1800. Default: 600 seconds. <TIME> Configures the minimum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra max-interval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command do?

The IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command adds static IPv6 client source binding information to the switch IPv6 binding database. Although DHCPv6 snooping is often used to dynamically populate the binding database, this command is available for manually adding entries to the switch IPv6 binding database. NOTE Statically configured IPv6 binding information supersedes any dynamically collected binding information for the same client. The no form of this command removes the specified binding that was statically configured with the IPv6 source-binding command. The no form has no effect on bindings that were dynamically configured with DHCPv6 snooping. Specifies the ID of an existing VLAN on which the client is conn ected. Range: 1 to 4094. <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR> <MAC-ADDR> <IFNAME> Specifies the client IPv6 address. Syntax: no IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command specifies the maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives. When a new advertisement arrives, hold time is reset. Hold time must be greater than or equal to the maximum advertisement interval. Therefore, if the hold time for an advertisement expires, the host can reasonably conclude that the router interface that sent the advertisement is no longer available. The default hold time is three times the maximum advertisement interval. The no form of this command removes the specified maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives and update it to the default value. Specifies the lifetime of router advertisements sent from this in terface. Range: 4 to 9000 seconds. Syntax: no ip irdp holdtime <TIME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command specifies the minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements. By default, this value is automatically set by the switch to be 75% of the value configured for maximum router advertisement interval. Use this command to override the automatically configured value. The no form of this command removes the specified minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements and reverts to the default value. Specifies the minimum time allowed between the sending of u nsolicited router advertisements. Range: 3 to 1800 seconds. De fault: 450 seconds (75% of the default value for maximum route r advertisement interval). <TIME>. Syntax: no ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the interface persona lag <NAME> command do?

The interface persona lag <NAME> command creates a persona LAG, a template for LAG (Link Aggregation Group) interfaces. A persona LAG defines configuration options that can be applied to multiple LAGs for consistency and simplified management. Only LAG interfaces can reference persona LAG templates. The persona LAG name cannot use reserved interface names or formats, such as vlan, lag, or loopback. Configurations defined in a persona LAG are valid only for LAG interfaces. The no form of this command deletes the persona LAG interface. Specifies the name of the persona LAG. <NAME>. Syntax: no interface persona lag <NAME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary] command do?

The ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary] command sets an IPv4 address and subnet mask to a LAG interface. One primary and up to 31 secondary address can be configured per interface. The no form of this command removes the IPv4 address from the interface. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.1 00. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Specifies a secondary IP address. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK> secondary. Syntax:
- no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary]
- ip address (interface lag) 1639.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show lldp neighbor-info mgmt command do?

The show lldp neighbor-info mgmt command displays information about neighboring devices connected to the OOBM interface. Displays LLDP neighbor information in structured JSON format. This command provides the same information as the standard show lldp neighbor-info command, but it prints the output in JSON format. Shows global LLDP statistics or statistics for a specific interface. Specifies an interface. Format: member/slot/port. Public show lldp statistics 1723 Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Shows LLDP statistics for the OOBM interface. Shows the LLDP TLVs that are configured for send and receive. Syntax: show lldp neighbor-info mgmt.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication console-login-attempts command do?

The aaa authentication console-login-attempts command for the console interface (channel) only, enables console login attempt limiting. If the number of failed console login attempts equals the configured threshold, the user is locked out for the configured duration. The no form of this command disables console login attempt limits. NOTE If you enable the lockout using this command and also enable the SSH, REST, and Telnet lockout using command aaa authentication limit-login-attempts, and then enter too many consecutive wrong passwords, you may become locked out, and will have to wait for the configured lockout time to elapse before logging in on any interface. NOTE This console login attempt limiting feature is only available when not using remote authentication through AAA servers (TACACS+ or RADIUS) on any interface. Syntax: aaa authentication console-login-attempts <ATTEMPTS> console-lockout-time.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command do?

The aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command enables/disables privilege elevation for the users in an administrators group. When enabled, an administrative user is provided lower privilege access upon successful switch login. The user must execute an enable command and re-authenticate to elevate their privilege to administrator (the administrator level access with ‘#’ prompt). Executing the disable command revokes an administrator privilege and returns the user to the lower privilege access level (the operator level access ‘>’ prompt). The no form of this command disables privilege elevation for the administrator user group. Syntax: no aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication minimum-password-length <LENGTH> command do?

The aaa authentication minimum-password-length <LENGTH> command enables minimum password length checking. Existing passwords shorter than the minimum length are unaffected. Length checking does not apply to ciphertext passwords. Length checking applies both to local and remote authentication. The no form of this command disables minimum password length checking. Public aaa authentication minimum-password-length 1738 Specifies the minimum password length. Range: 1 to 32. <LENGTH>. Syntax: no aaa authentication minimum-password-length <LENGTH>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show macsec policy command do?

The show macsec policy command shows information for one or all MAC sec policies. <MACSEC-POLICY-NAME> Specifies the MAC sec policy name. Range: 1 to 128 alphanumer ic characters including only the three special characters "." (peri od), "-" (hyphen), and "_" (underscore). Specifies the interface(s) for which to show MAC sec selftest in formation. <IFRANGE>. Syntax: show macsec policy [<MACSEC-POLICY-NAME>].

Shows MAC sec status information for all MAC sec-enabled interfaces or a specific interface or interface range. interface <IF-RANGE> Specifies one or more interfaces for which MAC sec status infor mation is to be shown. detailed Specifies that detailed status information is to be shown. Assigns an IPv4 or IPv6 default gateway to the management interface. An IPv4 default gateway can only be configured if a static IPv4 address was assigned to the management interface. messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 mld snooping command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping command this command configures the drop unknown mode. While MLD snooping is enabled, the traffic will be forwarded only to ports that initiate an MLD request for multicast. Drop unknown mode can be a filter across all VLANs (vlan-shared) or per VLAN (exclusive-vlan). The default configuration is vlan-shared. The no form of this command configures the drop unknown mode on the switch to the default vlan-shared. Required: Enable shared VLAN filter on the switch. vlan-exclusive Required: Enable exclusive drop unknown filter per VLAN. This command delays the refresh for some IGMP or MLD protocol-related values. When this command is enabled, IGMP/MLD last_reporter value, source, group, or querier uptime, and the create time and expiry time values will be updated based on the configured timer values. Syntax: IPv6 mld snooping drop-unknown {vlan-shared | vlan-exclusive}.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the MLD join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command disables the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Specifies the name of the ACL. <ACL-NAME> NOTE If the access list is configured for both L2 VLA N and L3 VLAN, the L3 VLAN configuration will be applied. Public IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list 1924. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans command enable MLD snooping on all VLANs. This configuration setting enables MLD snooping on all existing VLANs, as well as on any VLANs that are created after the IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans command is issued. When MLD snooping is enabled globally, MLD snooping can not be configured on individual VLANs within the VLAN context of the command-line interface. The no form of this command disables global MLD snooping, allows MLD snooping to remain enabled at the VLAN level, and allows the configuration of VLAN-level MLD snooping settings. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans.

Public IPv6 mld snooping auto vlan 1927 Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as an auto port. Specifies the number of a single VL AN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by c ommas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [auto vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

The no form of this command removes blocked ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a blocked port. Specifies the number of a single V LAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 6 0). This command enables the port to learn group information on receiving topology change notification. The no form of this command disables fastlearn on the ports. Required: 1/1/1-1/1/2, ports to be configured as fastlearn por ts. Syntax: IPv6 mld snooping fastlearn <port-list>.

Specifies the number of a single VLAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). Public IPv6 mld snooping fastleave vlan 1931. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>] command configures the given ports in forced fastleave mode. The no form of this command disables forced fastleave configuration on the ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a forced fastleave port. Specifies the number of a single VLAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, sepa rated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (1 0-40, 60). With forced fastleave enabled, MLD speeds up the process of blocking unnecessary multicast traffic to a switch port that is connected to multiple end nodes. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command this command enables AOS-CX leaf devices (access-layer switches that connect directly to servers, storage, and other endpoints) to unconditionally forward multicast traffic to all VTEPs that do not support the exchange of MLD reports over the fabric. By default, when MLD snooping is enabled in AOS-CX, multicast traffic is selectively forwarded only to VTEPs connected to have interested hosts. This is achieved by exhanging MLD reports over VXLAN tunnels where each VTEP processes the remote reports and uses the membership information to forward multicast traffic efficiently, conserving fabric bandwidth. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all.

syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show mpls forwarding command do?

The show mpls forwarding command shows the MPLS forwarding table. Shows the range of MPLS labels allocated for use in static LSP bindings and the range of labels currently used by static LSP bindings. Shows information about all MPLS LDP bindings. Shows information about discovered LDP peers. Specifies the peer MPLS LDP router ID in x.x.x.x format, where x is a decimal value from 0 to 255. Public show mpls ldp discovery 2035 Shows graceful restart parameters and status. Shows information about LDP neighbors in the current session(s). The reconnect and recovery time are the times advertised by the peer device. Configures MPLS static Label Switched Paths (LSP). The no form of this command removes all static LSP configurations including label range allocation and static LSP binding. Syntax: show mpls forwarding [detail].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show spanning-tree mst interface command do?

The show spanning-tree mst interface command shows MSTP configurations for the given instance ID with corresponding port details. Specifies the MSTP instance number. Range: 0 to 4094. <INSTANCE-ID> <IFNAME> vsx-peer Specifies an interface. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Shows spanning tree port summary information. Shows spanning tree root summary information. Enables the spanning tree protocol on the switch. The no form of this command disables the spanning tree protocol on the switch. Enables the bpdu filter for the interface. The BPDU filter feature allows control of spanning tree participation on a per-port basis. Syntax: show spanning-tree mst <INSTANCE-ID> interface <IFNAME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command do?

The ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command enables the router to forward multicast data received on the VXLAN L3VNI fabric to this interface, regardless of whether a multicast join was received on this interface or not. This allows the interface to be in the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers. This command must be enabled on the VSX VXLAN routers to support multicast receivers over ROP or P2P SVI extensions. This command can be enabled on a transit L3 peering between VSX peers. This command is optional when the uplink is an MCLAGsVI. The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface. NOTE • An IP address must be configured on the interface and pim-sparse must be enabled. • This command must be enabled only on one interface per VRF. Syntax: no ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor command do?

The ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor command once configured, the router processes IGMP/MLD and PIM joins received on this interface regardless of its DR or Prime Neighbor role. The command must be enabled for VSX VXLAN leaf switches for both L2 and L3 extensions. This allows for the interface to be in the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers. Public ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor 2136 The no form of the command disables the vsx-virtual-neighbor on the interface. NOTE This command is applicable for normal SVI interfaces and L2 VNI mapped SVI interfaces. It is valid for VXLAN-enabled VLANs only and has no effect on non-VXLAN-enabled VLANs. Syntax: no ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID> command do?

The ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID> command sets a key as trusted. When NTP authentication is enabled, the switch only synchronizes with time servers that transmit packets containing a trusted key. The no form of this command removes the trusted designation from a key. Specifies the identification number of the key to set as trusted. Range: 1 to 65534. Public ntp trusted-key 2288. Syntax: no ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf dead-interval command do?

The ip ospf dead-interval command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead if no hello packet is received on the OSPF interface. The no form of this command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead, to the default for the OSPF interface. The default value is 40 seconds (generally 4 times the hello packet interval). Specifies the time interval for the dead interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 40. <INTERVAL> Sets the time interval between OSPF hello packets for the OSPF interface. The no form of this command sets the time interval OSPF hello packets to the default of 10 seconds for the OSPF interface. Specifies the time interval for the hello interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 10. <INTERVAL> Sets the key chain for md5 authentication. Syntax: ip ospf dead-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf message-digest-key md5 command do?

The ip ospf message-digest-key md5 command sets the md5 message digest authentication key. If the md5 key is given in ciphertext, it will be decrypted and applied to the protocol. The no form of this command deletes the md5 authentication key. Specifies the md5 key ID. Range: 1 to 255. <KEY-ID> {ciphertext | plaintext} Selects the md5 key format. Specifies the md5 authentication key. <KEY> NOTE When the authentication key is not provided on the command line, plaintext key prompting occurs upon pressing Enter. The entered key characters are masked with asterisks. Configures the OSPFv2 network type for the interface. Public ip ospf network 2343 The no form of this command sets the network type for the interface to the system default which is broadcast network. Syntax: ip ospf message-digest-key <KEY-ID> md5 [{ciphertext | plaintext} <KEY>].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip ospf lsdb command do?

The show ip ospf lsdb command shows the OSPF link state database summary for different OSPF LSAs (Link State Advertisement). Use the parameters to get information for a particular LSA. Enter an OSPF process ID to display general OSPF information for a particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. <PROCESS-ID> adv-router {<ROUTER-ID>| self} Select to display link states for a particular advertising router. S pecify either a Router ID of the advertising router or specify sel f to show self-originated link states. area <AREA-ID> Select to display information filtered for the specified area in on e of the following formats. • OSPF area identifier in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a d ecimal number from 0 to 255. • OSPF area identifier in decimal format. Value: 0 to 4294967 295. Syntax: show ip ospf [<process-id>] lsdb.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip ospf routes command do?

The show ip ospf routes command displays OSPF routing information. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK> <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> protected Specify an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a deci mal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF neighbor informatio n for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to display neighbor information for all VRFs. Specify the name of a VRF. Default: default. Selects a protected route. Specifying an IPv4 address will select protected route for that prefix. Displays OSPF statistics. <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF neighbor informatio n for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Syntax: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] routes.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip ospf statistics interface command do?

The show ip ospf statistics interface command displays OSPF statistics for the OSPF-enabled interfaces. <PROCESS-ID> <INTERFACE-NAME> all-vrfs Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF-enabled interface statistics information on the specified OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to display information only for the specified interface. Select to display OSPF-enabled interface statistics informatio n for all VRFs. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specify the name of a VRF. Default: default. Displays the current state and parameters of the OSPF virtual links. <PROCESS-ID> brief all-vrfs Enter an OSPF process ID to display information on the OSPF vi rtual links for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to display brief overview information for the OSPF virtual links. Select to display OSPF virtual links information for all VRFs. Syntax: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] statistics interface [<INTERFACE-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command runs the OSPFv3 protocol on the interface for the area specified. Public IPv6 ospfv3 area 2449 To move an interface to a new area, unmap the existing area and then associate a new area with the interface. The no form of this command disables OSPF on the interface and removes the interface from the area. Interfaces which have an IP address configured on the network or in a subset of the network, stop participating in the OSPF protocol Specifies the area ID is one of the following formats. • Area ID in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal num ber from 0 to 255. • Area ID as a decimal value. Range: 0-4294967295. Specifies the OSPFv3 process ID. Range: 1 to 65535. <AREA-ID> <PROCESS-ID> Configures IPSec AH authentication. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 authentication ipsec spi <SPI-INDEX>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-dense hello-holdtime command do?

The ip pim-dense hello-holdtime command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. If not configured, the implementation derives the value from the current hello-interval. Syntax: ip pim-dense hello-holdtime <holdtime-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-dense ip-addr command do?

The ip pim-dense ip-addr command enables the router to dynamically determine the source IP address to use for PIM packets sent from the interface or to use the specific IP address. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of any. Specifies an IP address as the source IP for the interface. <IP-ADDR-VALUE> any. Syntax: ip pim-dense ip-addr {<IP-ADDR-VALUE> | any}.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip pim rpf (PIM-DM) command do?

The show ip pim rpf (PIM-DM) command displays PIM RPF details for the specified source or RP address in the given VRF and shows the nexthop and interface through which the shortest path to the source is available. It also displays if a PIM neighbor is present on the nexthop. Optionally, you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. <IP-ADDRESS> decimal number from 0 to 255. (Optional) Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), whe re x is a all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> vsx-peer Shows PIM RPF details for the given IP address. (Optional) Shows PIM interface information for all VRFs. (Optional) Shows PIM interface information for a particular VRF. If the <VRF-NAME> is not specified, it shows the default VRF information. Syntax: show ip pim rpf [<IP-ADDRESS>][all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 pim6-dense max-graft-retries command do?

The IPv6 pim6-dense max-graft-retries command configures the number of attempts the routing switch will retry sending the same graft packet to join a flow. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of 3 attempts. Specifies the number of retries for the routing switch to resend the graft packet. Default: 3 attempts. Range: 1-10. <INTERVAL-VALUE> Configures the override interval that gets inserted into the Override Interval field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets the value to the default of 2500 ms. Specifies the override interval of a LAN Prune Delay option in m s. Default: 2500 ms. Range: 500-6000. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-dense max-graft-retries <ATTEMPT-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip pim-sparse hello-interval command do?

The ip pim-sparse hello-interval command configures the frequency at which the router transmits PIM hello messages on the current interface. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of 30 seconds. Specifies the frequency at which PIM Hello messages are trans mitted on this interface. Range: 1 to 300. Default: 30. Public ip pim-sparse hello-interval 2879 NOTE • The default value (30 seconds) covers most deployment needs; change only for explicit convergence or timing requirements. • Lower values increase PIM Hello packet rate and control-plane / CPU load. • Neighbor Hold Time is derived automatically as 3.5 × hello-interval; reducing the interval tightens failure detection windows and heightens sensitivity to transient loss. Syntax: ip pim-sparse hello-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay command enables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. The default is enabled. With LAN-prune-delay enabled, the router informs downstream neighbors how long it will wait before pruning a flow after receiving a prune request. Other downstream routers on the same interface must send a join to override the prune before the LAN-prune-delay time to continue the flow. Prompts any downstream neighbors with multicast receivers continuing to belong to the flow to reply with a join. If no joins are received after the LAN-prune-delay period, the router prunes the flow. The propagation-delay and override-interval settings determine the LAN-prune-delay setting. Public IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay 2998 The no form of this command disables the LAN prune delay option. Syntax: no IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 pim6-sparse override-interval command do?

The IPv6 pim6-sparse override-interval command configures the override interval that gets inserted into the Override Interval field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets the value to the default of 2500 ms. Specifies the override interval of a LAN Prune Delay option in m s. Range: 500 to 6000. Default: 2500. <INTERVAL-VALUE> Configures the propagation delay that gets inserted into the LAN prune delay field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes currently configured value and sets to the default of 500 ms. Specifies the propagation delay value in ms. Range: 250 to 200 0. Default: 500. Public IPv6 pim6-sparse propagation-delay 3001 Configures the frequency at which the router will send periodic join or prune-interval messages. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-sparse override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show IPv6 pim6 pending command do?

The show IPv6 pim6 pending command shows the pending joins on a PIM router. Optionally you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Use this command to determine what flows are being requested on the PIM network. If data availability for a flow is expected, and a join for the flow is pending, the troubleshooting search moves to the source of that flow, since the routers are verified to be seeing the request for data. <GROUP-ADDR> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> vsx-peer Specifies a group address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxx x: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number fro m 0 to F. Shows information for all VRFs. Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. Syntax: show IPv6 pim6 pending [<GROUP-ADDR>] [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command configures an application to associate with a user-configured certificate. By default, all applications are associated with the default certificate "local-cert". Note that applications "captive-portal" and "hsc" are only available on certain platforms. The certificate associated with EST client is global to all EST profiles. Among the available applications, only dot1x-supplicant, est-client, and radsec-client are allowed to associate with device-identity certificate. Also note that device-identity certificate on a switch using a TPM of legacy version 1.2 only works with TLS version 1.2. 1. Syntax: no crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command creates a leaf certificate and changes to its context config-cert-<CERT-NAME>. If the specified leaf certificate exists, this command changes to its context. The first time the switch starts it creates a self-signed, default leaf certificate called local-cert. This certificate is used by any switch application that does not have an associated leaf certificate. The no form of this command deletes the specified leaf certificate. The default leaf certificate local-cert cannot be deleted. Specifies the name of a leaf certificate. Range: 1 to 32 alphanu meric characters (excluding "). <CERT-NAME>. Syntax: no crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show crypto pki est-profile command do?

The show crypto pki est-profile command shows a list of all configured EST profiles, or detailed information for a specific profile. Specifies the EST profile name. Range: Up to 32 alphanumeric c haracters (excluding "). <EST-NAME> Within the EST profile context, configures the URL of the certificate enrollment EST server. This is not configured by default. Any existing URL is replaced by this command. The no form of this command removes the EST server URL within the selected EST profile. The removal of the URL does not affect the TA profiles and enrolled certificates from the EST server. Specifies the EST server URL. Range: Up to 192 characters. Syntax: show crypto pki est-profile [<EST-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator auth-method eap-radius command do?

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator auth-method eap-radius command configures the authentication mechanism used to control access to the network. The configured authentication method will be used to authenticate 802.1X clients. The no form of the command resets the authentication mechanism to the default, eap-radius. Public aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator... 3171 a a a a u t h e n t i c a t i o n p o r t - a c c e s s d o t 1 x a u t h e n t i c a t o r... messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth command enables cached reauthentication on a port. Cached reauthentication allows 802.1X reauthentications to succeed when the RADIUS server is unavailable. Users already authenticated retain their currently assigned RADIUS attributes. The no form of the command disables the cached reauthentication on a port. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth.

command: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth
syntax: no aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth-period command configures the period during which an authenticated client, which has failed to reauthenticate because the RADIUS server is unreachable, remains authenticated. The no form of the command resets the cached reauthentication period to the default, 30 seconds. Specifies the cached reauthentication period (in seconds). Defa ult: 3600. The no form of the command resets the discovery period to the default, 30 seconds. Specifies the discovery period (in seconds). Default: 30. Range: 1 to 65535. <PERIOD> Configure the maximum size in bytes of an EAP-TLS fragment encoded in a single RADIUS request packet. messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth command enables cached reauthentication on a port. Cached reauthentication allows MAC reauthentications to succeed when the RADIUS server is unavailable. Users who are already authenticated, retain their currently assigned RADIUS attributes. The no form of the command disables cached reauthentication. Configures the period during which an authenticated client, which has failed to reauthenticate because the RADIUS server is unreachable, remains authenticated. The no form of the command resets the cached reauthentication period to the default, 3600 seconds. Public aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-rea... 3308 a a a a u t h e n t i c a t i o n p o r t - a c c e s s m a c - a u t h c a c h e d - r e a... Specifies the cached reauthentication period (in seconds). Defa ult: 3600. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth-period <PERIOD>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication port-access mac-auth reauth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access mac-auth reauth command enables periodic MAC reauthentication of authenticated clients on the port. The no form of the command disables periodic MAC reauthentication on the port. Configures the period after which MAC authenticated clients must be reauthenticated on the port. You must first enable MAC reauthentication on the port before configuring the MAC reauthentication period. The no form of the command resets the MAC reauthentication period to the default, 3600 seconds. Specifies the MAC reauthentication period (in seconds). Default: 3600. Range: 1 to 4294967295. messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ptp time-property command do?

The show ptp time-property command shows PTP clock-time properties for the PTP device. [vsx- peer] Displays VSX peer switch information. (8360 Switches only). Shows PTP running configuration related information. Sets the transport protocol for PTP packets. In the case of IPv4, the UDP check-sum is reset. There is no default transport-protocol. The no form of this command disconnects the clock from its source. ethernet Specifies the Ethernet (Layer 2) transport protocol. ip Specifies the IPv4 transport protocol. udp Specifies the UDP transport protocol. Specifies the transport layer port. Range: 1-65355. The defaul t is 4739. <UDP-PORT>. Syntax: show ptp time-property [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos egress-rewrite-map local-priority <PRIORITY-RANGE> {cos <PCP-VALUE>} command do?

The qos egress-rewrite-map local-priority <PRIORITY-RANGE> {cos <PCP-VALUE>} command configures a system-wide mapping from internal local priority to an 802.1Q VLAN priority code point (PCP) or DSCP value. The qos egress-rewrite cos command allows the PCP value to be rewritten on egress, and the qos egress-rewrite dscp command allows the DSCP value to be rewritten on egress. The local priority associated with a packet affects only its behavior within the switch. By default, it does not influence the priority value written to the packet upon transmission. This command, along with its no form, accepts a single local priority, a range of local priorities, or a comma-separated list of local priorities and ranges. This provides a convenient method for configuring Public qos egress-rewrite-map 3500 multiple entries in the map. Syntax: no qos egress-rewrite-map local-priority <PRIORITY-RANGE> {cos <PCP-VALUE>}.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos queue-profile <NAME> command do?

The qos queue-profile <NAME> command creates a new QoS queue profile and switches to the config-queue context for the profile. Or, if the specified QoS queue profile exists, this command switches to the config-queue context for the profile. A queue profile maps queues to local-priority values. Each profile has one to eight queues numbered 0 to 7. The larger the queue number, the higher its priority during transmission scheduling. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS queue profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied can be removed. Public qos queue-profile 3502 Specifies the name of the QoS queue profile to create or config ure. Range: 1 to 64 alphanumeric characters, including period (.), underscore (_), and hyphen (-). <NAME>. Syntax: no qos queue-profile <NAME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos threshold-profile <NAME> command do?

The qos threshold-profile <NAME> command creates a QoS threshold profile and switches to the config-threshold context for the profile. If the specified threshold profile exists, this command switches to the config-threshold context for the existing profile. The threshold profile determines the action to take when a threshold is exceeded for each queue. A threshold profile is composed of up to 7 queues, numbered from 0-6. Each queue defines the action to take when buffer utilization exceeds a specific threshold. Configure queues with the command queue. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS threshold profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied to an interface can be removed. Specifies the name of the QoS threshold profile to create or co nfigure. Syntax: no qos threshold-profile <NAME>.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show qos trust command do?

The show qos trust command shows the global QoS trust settings, or the factory default settings. Shows the factory default QoS trust settings. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Displays the mapping of PCP to DSCP values used to populate the tunnel IP header when encapsulating non-IP packets. Displays the global state of the queue monitoring feature. The output includes the global enable/disable status, the memory consumed by the feature, the statistics being collected, and the interfaces currently enabled for monitoring. Syntax: show qos trust [default] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the radius-server service-type in-access-request command do?

The radius-server service-type in-access-request command this configuration will include the Service-Type RADIUS attribute in Access-Request packets sent to a RADIUS server. The value of the attribute will always be NAS-Prompt-User. The no form of this command disables this feature. Syntax: no radius-server service-type in-access-request.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the radius-server status-server interval <10-86400> command do?

The radius-server status-server interval <10-86400> command configures the time interval in seconds to send the status server requests to the RADIUS server. The no form of this command configures the default time interval, 300 seconds. Specifies the status server time interval in seconds. Default: 30 0. <10-86400>. messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command creates RIP process if not already created and enters the router rip <PROCESS-ID> context for the VRF mentioned. If no VRF is mentioned, a default is used. Only one RIP process is allowed per VRF. The no form of this command deletes the RIP instance for the VRF. If no VRF is mentioned the default is deleted. Specifies name of the RIP process ID. Range: 1-63. <PROCESS-ID> vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies VRF name. Public RIP v2 Configuration commands 3718. Syntax: no router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip rip command do?

The ip rip command enables RIP process on an interface. The no form of this command deletes the RIP process from an interface. ip rip <PROCESS-ID> Specifies RIP process ID. Range: 1-63. all-ip ip-address. Syntax: ip rip <PROCESS-ID> {all-ip | ip-address}.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the clear ip rip statistics command do?

The clear ip rip statistics command clears RIP event statistics. Public RIP v2 clear commands 3734 Specifies RIP process ID. Range: 1-63 Clears statistics for all VRFs. Selects VRF to clear statistics for. Specifies VRF name. <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> Enables RIP process for RIP enabled IP address configured on interface. The no form of this command disables RIP process on interface. Disables RIP process for RIP enabled IP addresses configured on interface. The no form of this command enables RIP process on interface. Disables an interface from sending RIP packets for a specific IP address. The no form of this command enables interface for sending RIP packets for a specific IP address. Public send disable (rip) 3738 Disables interface from receiving RIP packets for a specific IP address. Syntax: clear ip rip [<PROCESS-ID>] statistics [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>].
